caption:
The Ferrari Makes It Even More Aesthetic 🫠
.
.
📹 charles_leclerc & antoine 
.
.
The Scuderia Ferrari star began a new chapter away from the lights of Formula 1. From the streets of Monaco to the grand stages of the world, Leclerc has built his legacy through passion, resilience, and relentless determination. Now, alongside the roar of V6 engines and the pursuit of world championships, he steps into a new journey — one defined by love and commitment.
For Ferrari fans, this feels like watching the “Prince of Scuderia” grow beyond the cockpit. The same focus and loyalty he shows every race weekend are now part of his personal story. While he continues chasing glory in red, today is about something bigger than podiums — it’s about partnership, promises, and a lifetime ahead.
A special moment in the life of one of Formula 1’s brightest stars. ❤️
.
.

caption: One eye contact away

caption:
On June 9, 1963, Jim Clark pulled off one of the most legendary drives in Formula 1 history at the Belgian Grand Prix in Spa-Francorchamps. Behind the wheel of his Lotus 25, powered by a 1.5L Climax V8, Clark started eighth on the grid in pouring rain-conditions that turned the already treacherous 14.1 km Spa layout into a high-speed slip ‘n slide. Within the first lap, he sliced through the field to take the lead and never looked back, finishing over 4 minutes ahead of second place. He even slowed near the end, thinking he might’ve gone off course because he couldn’t see any other cars.

What made this win even more astonishing was Clark’s complete control in chaos. Spa in the ‘60s was unforgiving-narrow roads, zero runoff, and blind corners bordered by trees and houses. Most drivers feared it in the wet, but Clark made it look effortless, showcasing his instinctive feel and unmatched car control. That race not only solidified his reputation as a rain master, but also marked one of his seven wins that season-enough to clinch his first World Championship.

via f1moti
author: 5secondpenalty

author: 5secondpenalty

caption:
At the 1991 British Grand Prix at Silverstone, one of Formula 1’s most iconic moments unfolded after the checkered flag. Nigel Mansell, driving for Williams-Renault, had just secured victory in front of his home crowd in the FW14—a car powered by a naturally aspirated 3.5-liter V10 producing around 700 hp and featuring active suspension, a pioneering technological advancement at the time. As Ayrton Senna’s McLaren-Honda ran out of fuel on the cooldown lap, Mansell pulled over and offered him a ride back to the pits, with Senna perched on the sidepod of the winning car.

This spontaneous gesture became an enduring image of sportsmanship and mutual respect between two fierce rivals. While the FW14 would go on to dominate the following year, this particular race is remembered less for technical benchmarks and more for the human moment that transcended the competition. It captured the spirit of an era when legends shared not only the track, but also rare displays of camaraderie in a sport defined by intensity.
